content.full_text:
Re
Farn PolrrrcAr. PnACTTcES CovrrvrrssroN
428 J Street. Suite 620. Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660. Fax (916) 322-0886
December 31,2002
Nancy Aaberg
Assistant Superintendent of
Instructional Services
Yuba City Unified School Diskict
750Palora Avenue
Yuba City, CA 95991
Your Request for fnformal Assistance
Our File No.I-02-351
Dear Ms. Aaberg:
QUESTTONS
1. Are you barred qnder the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act from
accepting appointment to the Curriculum Development and Supplemental Materials
Commission ("Curriculum Commission"), based on your receipt of royalty payments
from two publishers of educational materials?
I Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appearat Title 2, sections
18109-18-997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not prwide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 83 1 14; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)
3 State and local agencies are authorized to adop[ iut"-"ntr of incompatible activities that
prohibit their employees from engaging in any outside employment, activities, or enterprises which are
inconsistent or in conflict with their duties as agency employees, or which are subject to control or approval
by their state or local agency employer. (Government Code sections 19990 and 7126, et seq.) This iiea of
law is outside the jurisdiction of the Commission and we offer no advice thereon. You may wish to check
with the State Board of Education's counsel or the state Attomey General's office regarding these laws.
This leffer is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the o'Act').I Since your request does not
identiff a specific decision or proceeding and seeks our general guidance, we are treating
your request as one for informal assistance.' Our informal assistance is based on the facts
presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission ("Commission') does not act as a
finder of fact when it provides informal assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops.
T)3


File No. I-02-351
Page No. 2
2. Will the two publishers providing you with royalty payments be economic
interests to you, for purposes of determining whether you, in your prospective capacity as
a member of the Curriculum Commission will have a potentially disqualiffing conflict of
interest?
CONCLUSIONS
1. No. There are no provisions of the Act that prohibit an individual from
accepting appointrnent to public office. However, once holding office, a public official is
subject to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act with respect to the governmental
decisions he or she may make, participate in making, or influence.
2. College Preparatory Math ("CPM') is a source of income to you aggregating
$500 or more for the year 2002. As such, it is an economic interest to you that potentially
disqualifies you from making, participating in making, or influencing any governmental
decisions of the Curriculum Commission that may have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on CPM.
FACTS
You are an appointee by the State Board of Education ("Board,) to the
Curriculum Commission, to serve a four-year term commencing January l, 2003. You
have not yet acceptedthat appointment. The Curriculum Commission, established by
Education Code Sections 33530, et seq., is composed of 13 public mernbers appointed by
the Board, a member of the State Assembly, amember ofthe State Senate, and three
public members, one of which is appointed, respectively, by the Governor, the Senate
Committee on Rules, and the Speaker of the Assernbly. Members are volunteers who
receive no compensation other than actual and necessary travel expenses. Members of
the Curriculum Commission are designated officials under the Board's conflict of interest
code.
You are currently an assistant superintendent of instructional services for the
Yuba City Unified School District. Approximately 20 years ago, you were a contributing
author to a publication for which you received royalties from Prentice Hall (later
subsumed by Pearson Education, Inc.) ("Prentice Hall'). Approximately l0 years ago,
you worked as an editor on apublication for which you received royalties from College
Preparatory Math ("CPM'). You received royalties from these publishers on an ongoing
basis.
During 2001, the royalties you received from Prentice Hall totaled $338. you
received $1,244 in royalties for the same calendar year from CPM. In a telephone
conversation with the Commission's staff held on December 23,2002, you stated that
your royalties during 2002totaled less than $500 from Prentice Hall, and more than $500
from CPM.a You have disclosed these circumstances fully to the Board. You indicate
a In this telephone conversation you also clarified your request for advice to include question 2,
above.


File No. I-02-351
Page No. 3
that should you decide to accept appointment to the Curriculum Commission, you will
permanently renounce the receipt of any future royalties from CPM and Prentice Hall in
connection with your prior activities.
AI\ALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, particrpating in making, or
otherwise using his orher official position to influence a govemmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest. The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard
analysis for deciding whether an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest
(regulation 18700, subdivisions (bxl) - (8), which is discussed below. The general rule,
however, is that a conflict of interest may occur whenever a public official makes,
participates in making, or influences a govemmental decision which may materially
affect one or more of his or her economic interests.
Public officials do not have a conflict of interest on general principles; a conflict
of interest arises, if at all, in context of the official's involvement in governmental
decisions which affect the official's economic interests. Holding a position on the
Curriculum Commission does not, in itself create a potential disquali$ring conflict of
interest under the Act. (Foster Advice Letter, No. A-02-125.)
1. & 2. Are you a public official making, participating in making, or
influencing a governmental decision?
The conflict-of-interest prohibitions apply only to public officials. We previously
advised that mernbers of the Curriculum Commission are public officials that will make,
participate in making, or influence govemmental decisions. (Brown Advice Letter, No.
A-00-112.)
3. What are your economic interests?
The economic interests that might give rise to a conflict of interest are defined in
regulations 18703-18703.5. The particular potential economic interests identified in your
request for advice are Prentice Hall and CPM.5 In this regard., a public official has an
economic interest in any source of income, including promised income, which aggregates
to $500 or more within 12 months prior to a decision at issue. (Section 87103(c);
regulation 18703.3.)
5 In addition to the economic interests separately listed in section 87103, a public official always
has an economic interest in his or her personal finances, and may have a conflict of interest in any decision
foreseeably resulting in an increase or decrease in the personal expenses, income, assets or liabilities of the
official or his or her immediate family, in the amount of $250 or more over a l2-month period. (Regulation
18703.s.)


File No. I-02-351
Page No. 4
You indicate that your royalty income from Prentice Hall for the year 2002 will
be less than $500.6 Thus, Prentice Hall will not be a source of income to you, for
purposes of defining your economic interests under the conflict-of-interest provisions of
the Act. On the other hand, you state that your royalty income from CPM for the year
2002will equal or exceed $500. Thus, at least irutiallyT, CPM is a source of income that
is an economic interest to you, for purposes of the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Act.
Whether this interest disqualifies you from participatingfrom a particular decision
of the Curriculum Commission depends on whether the decision will have areasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on CPM, distinguishable from the effect upon the
public generally. To assist you in making this determination in the context of a particular
decision you may face as a member of the Curriculum Commission, we enclose the
Commission's publication "Can I Vote?" which summarizes the Commission's eight-step
standard conflict of interest analysis.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
L. Glick
Counsel, Legal Division
Enclosures
KLGje
I:\Adviceltrs\02-351
6 Note that the source of income rule in section 87103(c) is not based on the amount received in a
calendar year, but on the amount an official receives over the l2-month period immediately preceding a
particular governmental decision.
' The $500 threshold for this pu{pose is an aggregate amount. Thus, if the royalties you received
from CPM during 2002 were paid on a periodic basis, it is only when the rolling l2-month aggregation of
those payments, dating backwards from the date of aparticular govemmental decision, equals or exceeds
$500 that CPM remains a potentially disqualifying source of income to you.
B
